sentences.text: Yes.
My name is Ruth Crane.
R-U-T-H C-R-A-N-E.
My name at birth was Ruth, R-U-T-H, Siegreich, S-I-E-G-R-E-I-C-H.
That's the 11th month.
I'll be next month 69.
Siemianowice.
S-I-E-M-I-N-O-W-I-C-E.
Poland.
Yes.
My mother's Zosie.
Yes.
Z-O-S-I-E.
My dad, Julek.
I'm telling you the Polish names, yes?
Julek, which is J-U-L-E-K.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My sister, Carmela.
C-A-R-M-E-L-A.
And the older one was Lilka, which is L-I-K-- L-I-L-K-A.
I was.
Lilka.
She was born in April 1934.
Wait a second.
She was seven.
Was it?
Yes, that's right.
Close to 1932 anyhow.
The other one was 1934.
Carmela.
Yes.
Yes.
The first one when I was a child, is-- it was Number 16 Bytomska.
B-Y-T-O-M-S-K-A.
And when I turned about-- when I was about seven years of age-- of age, we-- we've moved to Pilsudskigo, which was 11.
P-R-- P-I-L-S-U-D-S-K-I-G-O.
Yes.
I think that because it was closer to my parents' business.
Yes.
No.
No.
It was in a block of flats.
This is how most-- most people lived in Siemianowice.
Absolutely.
Yes.
Yes.
It was a very beautiful flat.
We-- the three of us, the three girls, had an enormous bedroom.
We slept all together.
And as a matter of fact, we had-- it was huge.
And in the back of it-- or in the front of it, really, there were windows all surrounding-- all around there were windows.
And it was-- you had to go up two steps.
It was a-- where you could perform.
It was like a little stage that you-- it was like a little stage that you had to climb up.
And this is where we were encouraged to perform.
The floor was green, was painted green.
And I think anything that could stimulate us, enrich our lives, it happened there.
Yes, absolutely.
Yes.
Little-- little poems, little Polish songs.
Then at school, you had to learn poetry.
So you know, you read it a couple of times and there it was.
Came easy to most of us.
So-- yes.
So it was very-- it was very structured, the life-- our little life was very structured and continuous.
You felt the continuity.
But it was very happy because I think children like myself were cherished, were respected, and were talked to.
So I think-- I thought that everybody lived like this, you know?
So I think I knew what was inside, and I knew what-- what-- what awaited me outside.
You were talking about performances in the-- in your bedroom.
Yes.
I don't think so, that I would want to do it right now.
But what I might do later on, I might do something later on.
OK?
If you don't mind, yes.
I think most of the time we conversed in Polish, although my parents did occasionally in Hebrew.
My mother, particularly, spoke French fluently.
She knew Latin.
She knew German, Russian, and Yiddish, of course.
Somehow, my parents were not profound Yiddishists.
But by my grandparents, I was addressed most of the time in Yiddish.

So I think that-- and my mother felt in particular, as we lived very close to the German border-- so when you went outside people were conversing and the languages were [NON-ENGLISH], which-- [NON-ENGLISH].
Which was sort of half Polish, half Germans.
Their grandparents were German.
The parents were German too, really.
And the children had to attend Polish schools.
So their Polish somehow was very poor and inarticulate.
And I think that somehow-- I don't know how with the walls-- but somehow the children in this particular town-- after all, this is-- this is where I lived.
I think they were somehow high achievers, you know?
So I think that the parents made sure that their Polish was fluent.
So probably that's why they converse, many in Polish.
I don't think that like today, you let it all hang out and the parents discuss those things with the children.
I think then, it was just, you know, you were a child and you were loved and respected.
But things were just not explained to you, why you are doing certain things and what you are doing.
It was just in front of you, and it should be done.
And you didn't question it.
Yes.
It was on the first floor.
Yes.
It was a kitchen with a balcony.
And there was a room next to it where-- I wouldn't want to call it servant, call her servant.
But we had a lady with us who only worked in the kitchen.
Didn't do anything else.
Then we had--
Her name was Henia.
The last one that we had was Henia.
And she was Jewish, as a matter of fact.
And then it was a very large living room.
Then there was a passage.
On the right-hand side not far from the entrance was our bedroom.
On the left-hand side was the kitchen.
And there was another very big balcony leading out from the kitchen.
And there was what we call salon.
It was like living-- living room.
A very beautiful living room with those enormous windows.
And then there were three bedrooms.
One was a guest bathroom.
My parents had a bedroom, of course, a large one.
And we had this-- our bedroom.
And next to my parents' bedroom-- the facilities were really in my parents' bedroom.
And next to my parents' a bathroom there was like a-- what you would call today a study.
And there were all the books, most of the books that my mother or my-- both of my parents have cherished.
And there were just hundreds of them, and you just could get there and get lost there, you know.
And you were like in a different-- on a different planet.
And they were even all the telegrams.
I used to love to look all through the telegrams that my parents received when they got married, or when we got born-- when we were born, you know.
Things like that.
So--
I think the books would be in various languages.
I think there were a lot of books interpreted from French into Polish, which was very popular in Poland.
And there were-- there were sentences in French as well in-- in those books, where-- in where-- inverted commas, you know.
And I think I was encouraged to do this.
I don't remember that I was.
But I must have been, because then I could go to my mother and ask her.
But I thought that every mother is like this, you know?
So-- yes.
So-- and then there were-- there were those-- there were, of course, Polish, with-- from-- interpreted from French.
And there were in various other languages which was really-- there were some German books.
There were Hebrew books.
But I think mostly they were Polish.
I enjoyed reading books from-- about the French Revolution.
I was trying to think there is one book in particular that I have literally studied when I was in the ghetto.
And this is Emile-- Emile Zola's.
Emile Zola's, that was about a one man-- that was a true story, I think, and he was sent to the-- to-- to Devil Island.
And yes.
And I think it was Esterhazy.
It was somebody by the name of Esterhazy who accused him. maybe, it was as well-- it was made into a film, probably.
But most of the time-- I mean, anything if it had literary merit, I enjoyed reading it.
And I-- I-- I had a voracious-- as far as I can remember, voracious appetite for reading.
So this is-- this is how my-- how my-- how my whole family was.
So it was a way of life.
You didn't-- I didn't know anything else, yes.
Well, I went to-- the first school was the-- not far from Bytomska.
And this is what I would like to tell you a bit about, because till I-- before-- till before-- till before I started school, I was really very sheltered.
I led a very sheltered life as a small child, because I always went with an adult.
I never went unaccompanied by an adult.
And we happened to play, let's say, outside.
But it was in a yard.
So it was enclosed.
The yard was enclosed.
And we played there.
And I didn't go outside to the street, you know, just walk down the street by myself.
But once I had to start school, I had to sit for an exam because I was a little bit younger than the others.
And by the time I started school, I could read and write, because I had for about a year before a private teacher who prepared me for this.
Oh, I don't-- I couldn't say.
But I suppose about twice a week.
So by the time I started the first grade, there were no-- no preparatory, things like this.
I had to sit for an exam.
No, I don't.
No, I don't, really.
This is when I was five, so I don't really.
But there was one, she was-- she was a lady.
And I know that she wasn't very young, so she could have been middle aged.
Or to me, then, middle aged anyhow.
And I remember the first day at school vividly, because I was so excited.
I remember there was a photo of me taken with a cone, with something in the shape of a cone.
And with lollies and toys.
And I was so proud, I'm going to attend school.
And then the first day when I came in and they-- when they realized that I am Jewish, I got straightaway a [POLISH].
That was the first day.
Which was-- which to interpret it, really, you can't exactly.
But it meant that I'm Jewish, and it meant that I am filthy and that I am full of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all kinds of things crawling all over me.
[POLISH], it means, you know, that you had some wounds, like little wounds, you know, all over you.
And when I was-- when I was trying to sit down, we had benches not chairs.
And I was in the third row.
And there were two pins stuck into me straightaway on the first day.
Yes, yes.
On a daily basis.
Yes.
By children.
I mean, now I know that they were children-- now I know that there were children.
But then I didn't, you know?
Then the children didn't seem to me children because they were very hostile.

The children that I played with-- where we lived there was-- there could have been about 50 families around because the-- the way the flats were built, it was that the yard was in the middle and on the side were the flats.
So people lived, you know, all around.
And there was only downstairs across from us one printer, one Jewish family who had a-- a-- who had a printing sort of business.
But all the rest were non-Jews.
And somehow to them, they keep telling me, oh, you are not like-- like a Jewish girl.
You know, I didn't-- I didn't take any notice because sort of we were still friendly, you know?
We sort of liked each other.
I was their friend then.

But once I was outside walking the street, as I had to sometimes, by myself, as we were not driven to school, I didn't want to be-- I could have been not driven, but taken, by-- by horse-- by horses and-- and a buggy.
But I didn't.
I wanted to be just like everybody else.
I didn't want to be different.
And I thought, you know, that this will sort of allow me, really-- I wanted to be friends.
I wanted to make friends at school and things like that, you know.
Yes.
Yeah.
No.
No.
No.
I was the only one in that particular school because they were very selective.
In the next school, [POLISH] Pilsudskigo, there were three Jewish children in the whole school.
They just did not-- they try in a subtle way to-- to avoid explaining why not, but they really did not accept Jewish school-- Jewish children.
No.
No.
They-- they didn't really-- they did not pick when the teachers were around.
And I didn't go-- I-- I decide-- I didn't go crying to-- to the teacher because I-- I felt-- I somehow sensed it, that I would antagonize her further.
But I went to my mother.
Not straightaway, but after a while.
And I said to her, why?
I mean, in what way am I-- what did I do?
Did I do something wrong?
Why do they pick on-- why do they call me names?
And this is not just at school, because I had to walk about 25 minutes and then back.
I'm talking about the second school.
And the first one was not far.
And then I went to a Jewish school, which was another-- on the other side next to the shul, on the other side of-- of the town.
So altogether, I did about two-- nearly two hours walking there and back.
And my mother said to me right there and then, if I could go out and protect you from what awaits you there, each time I would do it.
If I could lie down my life for you and if I knew that it would change, I would do it.
But the thing is that at home, you are our princess.
But when you are outside, you are really on your own.
So you have to make up your mind you're going to take pride in who you are, or you're going to dislike it or hate it.
And you going to suffer further.
You have to think about your feelings.
And it's up to you, really.
So I decided somehow-- perhaps you know, in not such a-- I mean, I was too young, really, to philosophize or to intellectualize.
But somehow, I always loved my Jewishness.
I loved it, you know?
I felt such a sense of belonging.
But I wanted to be friends with others too.
I liked them too.
But somehow without knowing me, without giving me a chance, I was Jewish but it was not just myself.
There were close to 500 children in this town.
And there was--
--for as long-- close to 500 Jewish children.
By the time, as for as long as I can remember, as long as my memory reaches back, Hitler was already in power.
You see?
And then the burning of the books took place, and then the Nuremberg laws, you know.
All of this as they identified-- I mean, I'm generalizing, I know.
But this is how it was to me.
And I am speaking now about the perception of a child.
To me, they seemed to be so strong and so powerful, and I-- where I walked, I always felt that I walk, that this was not my territory.
That I was not really, you know, by the grace of God or whoever, that I walked those streets.
I mean, I-- I walked down those streets.
But this was not-- does not belong to me.
I was not welcome there.
As a matter of fact, for the first time when I came into Australia, I did not have to look over my shoulder.
But for as long as I remember, till then I always knew that I have to look in the back of me and in the front of me.
But again, a child adjust and adapts.
And this was the way-- this was our lifestyle.
And it was in the beginning very confusing because at home, that was this atmosphere that as children we were so cherished and so nurtured, and we felt so-- so important.
And outside, we were just nobody.
And I didn't mind to go down the street and to be a nobody.
But oh, no.
I mean, I was not allowed to, you know?
So I had to put up with this.
And after a while, if I walked with a friend, this sometimes we try to arrange this.
But as it was so far and we didn't talk on the phone like we do now-- we had the telephone, but I wouldn't think, you know, to ring to my friends because most of them didn't.
So we just-- if we just happened to meet by chance, it was better.
But if you were on your own, if you can imagine, to walk down the street and through-- to-- from far, I could see, oh, ho.
There aer about 10 or 12 of them.
Big fat bullies, you know?
So it was-- I somehow-- I don't know.
Perhaps something in my-- in that my stance, or something in the way I held myself.
That after a while it was not so painful.
And after a while, I was not as vulnerable.
What did the bullies do?
Called me names.
Pushed me.
Oh, no.
I wouldn't.
I didn't even want to know their names.
[NON-ENGLISH].
What they called me mainly, those names.
[POLISH].
You know.
That I am fat.
That I am ugly.
Anything they could think of.
But what I realize now-- I mean, it was so brutal, you know.
It was.
But what I realize, that they were really children, and this is what they've learned on their grandparents' knees.
So you-- you know, they've learned to-- to adore-- to worship Hitler.
They've learned all the, you know, warfare.
About all of this.

And I think they were children, again-- because they were really born in Poland and attended Polish schools, really they were very-- they didn't have that inner security of knowing the language, the Polish language fluently either.
Very strongly.
I mean, they were-- they were more than traditional.
They were religious.
Not in dress, but otherwise everything was observed.
And our life was governed by the Jewish laws.
It was, I think, the way the tone of the voice-- of their voices.
For instance, like my grandfather who had 18 grandchildren.
I think 18.
He was-- he-- we didn't live in the same town.
He lived about 3/4 of an hour away in Bedzin.
But he would come at least once a week and sit with me for at least three hours, two or three hours, just with me, and read to me.
Take the Siddur, interpret from the Siddur.
And I think that as he was interpreting to me, he was sort of weaving his own story into it as well.
And it was somehow-- the way I perceived it, that it was all about love and peace.
So really, he-- he instilled in me that feeling and the love for peace, for inner peace, particularly.
As he particularly-- as he was a very-- really a very gentle person.
He was very intelligent and very perceptive.
I realized later on-- because my mother was his daughter-in-law.
But somehow he never-- it never ceased to-- cease to amaze me the way he talked about my mother.
How important she was to him.
Icie Mayer.
Icie.
I-C-I-E-.
Mayer.
The way he spelled it with a-- M-A-Y-E-R. -E-R.
Siegreich.
Yes.
Yes.
Well, the synagogue was quite far from us.
We had to go-- I was there every day because I had to go to-- to my school next to it, that was next with the shul.
But it was only this one synagogue, and it was on-- on the other end of the town on the left hand side, which was quite far to go.
So I went very often when I went with my father.
So Friday night, Saturday.
And my father was coming-- I suppose that was-- that was one of the reasons why we shifted so he could come and say [HEBREW], you know.
So I-- in the morning, I watched him quite often.
I don't know if he made sure that I watch him, or it just happened like this.
I don't know.
But I know that I watched him with reverence, you know, and he was putting on-- on the tefillin.
And when he was, you know, putting on the band and putting on the tallit.
And it was such a ritual.
That I could see although it-- it repeated itself every day.
But I could see that he was focusing whilst he was putting it on.
I think everything else fell away.
He was-- he was really praying.
He was really doing it from-- from his heart, with his heart and with his mind.
And this is, I suppose, where my perception came where-- if-- whatever you do, you've got to do your best and you've got to do from your heart.
Just do it with your head is not good enough.
So this is what I perceived when he was putting on that tefillin.
And when my mother was lighting the candles, it was the same thing.
You know, I could sense because she was preparing-- she was around-- she created an atmosphere in preparing the Shabbat.
So when I stood next to her when she was lighting the candles, there was already an atmosphere prepared to welcome the Shabbat.
And I felt the peace descending on me.
So whatever happened during the-- the week, it just fell away.
And as a matter of fact, now when I talk about it, I've got a four-year-old granddaughter.
And when she comes here in the beginning and I lit the candles, and she put her little hand on my arm like this when I lit the candle.
And as-- I asked my daughter, Helen, what is she doing?
She said, Mom, this is what-- what she's being taught.
So she is putting the hand for her to feel it as well, for her to-- to-- to soak it up, you know?
So each time I-- I light the candle, she-- she will like guide my hand.
I wasn't doing this.
But-- but I felt-- I felt this.
Until today when I light the candles, I feel the same peace around me.
No matter what happened before in the home-- at home, no matter how it looks.
There is total peace for when I light the candles and for later on.
Good.
